S.F.

A Trip Book

San Francisco

Tap Next to begin

Golden Gate Bridge

That first glimpse of the bridge through the fog — the towers just barely poking through before the whole span pulled into view.

Completed in 1937, the bridge's Art Deco towers were painted "International Orange" partly because the color shows up so well against San Francisco's fog.

Alcatraz Island

From the water, the old cell house looks almost peaceful sitting out there — hard to picture it as the country's most notorious federal prison.

The federal penitentiary operated from 1934 to 1963 and the island now draws over a million visitors a year as a National Park Service site.

Fisherman's Wharf

Salty air, the smell of sourdough and crab, and the bark of sea lions somewhere down the pier — this stretch of the waterfront never quite settles down.

Fisherman's Wharf grew out of a 19th-century fishing fleet, and its famous sea lions didn't show up on the Pier 39 docks until 1989.

Lombard Street

Eight tight switchbacks of red brick and flowers, and a steady line of cars crawling down them one at a time — steeper in person than it ever looks in photos.

The famous crooked block was built in 1922 to make the natural 27% grade of the hill safely drivable.
confirm venue

City Skyline

A wide, hilly view of downtown from up high — the kind of overlook that makes you stop and just take in how the whole city is stacked across the hills.

San Francisco is built across more than 50 hills, which is a big part of why so many of its street-level views end up looking like postcards.
confirm venue

A Little Souvenir

Picked up somewhere along the way — a small keepsake to bring the trip home, the kind of thing that ends up living on a keyring for years.

San Francisco's cable cars have run since 1873 and are the last manually operated cable car system left in the world.

The End

A trip through the city by the Bay, six pages long — thanks for flipping through.
